All right, we're getting close to the end. We got some more bonus footage for you after this, but one more very important topic here, and that is what do you do with those reluctant few who have low standards but don't want to do anything about it? Do you know anybody like that? Hopefully it's not you. Now, hopefully you don't owe them to intimately, right? But hopefully there's something to employ you. Well, guess what? I'm going to show you now in this lesson how you even take those reluctant few and raise their standards. And by the way, this can apply to anybody, business, personal, right? Your own family members. Just remember this. As I explain this to Sarah, you always have to maintain your ethics here. Remember that. You don't want to use these strategies. They're powerful. So you're not going to use them to empower people. Use them to empower people, to help them live a better life, to get back that spark they once had that's vanished now because of insults that either they're fault or just simply random occurrences that cause them to feel so much pain, they're like, no, Moss, taunt to low my standard, kind of dumb things down on my own life, burn less bright, so at least feel okay. Now you're going to learn to wake those people up. Watch this and I'll see you on the backside. I'm sure you know there are people out there who have low standards and they don't really want to raise those standards, but they maybe need to raise those standards. So how do you raise those people's standards? That's a good question, right? Well, you know, we have something in life called leverage, right? And leverage is sort of that, like kind of that X factor. I have leverage over you. Well, if I don't have leverage over you or that person, well, it's very difficult to force them to make change. And it's almost really not even fair to try because you have to really be like a blunt force instrument, really almost not hurting someone, but like, you know, coming at them really hard. Here's what I would say. If it's someone in your family who you love and you care about, then you try to find leverage over that person. Try to find something. I don't mean it's a negative. I mean, in a positive way, positive leverage to be able to point out to them why their life is not really working as it is. You know, on some level, what is an intervention, a drug intervention is about leverage where everybody gets involved and say, you know what, the leverages were all cutting you off because without that, why would someone want to stop doing drugs? And you know, and they're at it, right? And I add that, by the way, where the leverage was my whole family got together and thank God they did, right? But when it comes to people with low standards who don't want to raise their standards, you have to first, is an ethical check here, which is okay. Are they nuts? Like are they just wrong? Like, you know, they say, I don't need to raise my standards. You can see their life is really a wreck and they'll complain about it. I don't, I like it. I'm what they talk out of one side of their mouth. Oh, no, everything's great the way it is. I can live on love alone. And they spend the rest of their life complaining about having no money and they can't pay their medical bills and everyone else is an idiot because they do have my medical back. That person, you want to find leverage over, right? So, you know, possible ways to really go about doing that are to listen, if a person is working for you, it has to be the threat of losing a job. I'm serious. You know, I absolutely would bring that to bear in a positive way. I don't believe in, I don't like to motivate by fear. I like to motivate by positivity. I like to catch people doing things right versus wrong, but in the real world of management and sales, sometimes you got to put the fear of God in somebody to get them to act, you know, and just the gut check there, the ethical check would be, is are you really empowering this person? So, I would say if someone is delusionally going through life with low standards and not caring about it and you see the pain that's causing them, then you owe it to that person to try to find leverage and then run the other strategies of pain and pleasure. But you have to have some leverage there. If they don't want to, it's like, you know, you can lead a horse to water, you can't make him drink, right? Well, guess what? Same thing is true with drug rehab. You know, people, you know, they go to these beautiful drug rehabs, Betty Ford, Hazeless, right? They jump over the wall. They're not ready. They're not getting sober anywhere. You can bring them to 20 rehabs, they'll just run, the lie, oh yeah, I'll get something to leave the next day they'd be using it, right? When do they change when they're ready? If they're ready to stop doing drugs, they'll get sober in the rooms of alcoholics and anonymous, right? Because they're ready. So, how do you interview when someone's not ready? Well, that's what leverage is about. You have to find something valuable to that person and basically hold it over their head, whether it's a job, a promotion, something, a loved one, if you're doing it to serve them. And that will be a distinction with that. So I think that all together though, I find it that in my life I do a lot better when I'm not trying to get people to do things they don't want to do. Even like the straight line system is about that on some level. Like, you know, we don't try to sell people things they don't want or need. I don't want to turn non-buyers into buyers. I want to turn prospective buyers who need my product, who want my product, who could afford my product, but are skeptical. I want to take those people and make them buyers and make them happy buyers and sell them again and again and again for the long term, right? So it's a very different thing than trying to shove a squab hanging around whole, low standards. I don't want to raise them, but I want to make you raise them. That's not going to end up so well. So I think you find those, those circumstances where yes, you know, someone is sort of in such denial and you owe it to them to give it a good college try, find leverage, run those pain and pleasure strategies, make them visualize. And also remember, so much of this is who do they associate with, who are their mentors and are they continuing to learn and grow? Something happens with the straight line because you're learning this new skill set. And it essentially almost automatically rewrite some of your belief systems because you save yourself, you know, you might not unconscionably say, I'm not the same person I used to be. I'm really great at communication now. So my past results don't equal my future results because I didn't know this stuff. See that was the secret to the kids at Stratton and all the millions of people I've trained I get these emails all the time. You've seen many of them. They say, it's like, how did I, it's like, how does this happen? They just started to say, never been successful. They're wildly successful with the straight line because it rewrites their belief systems, their identity, their own identity and the standard results from that identity. Because they say, wow, I own this new skill set. So the fact that I wasn't successful, I know why now. I was a terrible influencer. But now I can influence. So now I'm going to take action. They start working twice as hard. It's a self-proclaimed prophecy that feeds on itself and it feeds on its own. There you go. All right. There you go. So that's powerful stuff. Remember, leverage is only to be used for purposes of good. I am the first to admit I was an expert in finding leverage. And in my younger days, I did not always use it to empower people. I don't want you to do that. I personally would never do it again, but it's so powerful. Once you find leverage and I've given you some ways in there, there'll be more ways in the back end here.
